frameworks.name: categorical perception
frameworks.one_line: Cardinal axes act as perceptual categories that bias near-cardinal orientations away from them.

frameworks.name: signal-detection theory
frameworks.one_line: Visibility manipulation via duration and masking frames orientation readout under varying sensory noise.

frameworks.name: efficient coding
frameworks.one_line: Greater sensitivity near cardinals motivates anisotropic orientation representation in the visual system.

core_question: How does stimulus visibility modulate the illusory exaggeration of small deviations from cardinal orientations?
key_assumptions: Orientation perception is anisotropic with privileged cardinal axes
Visibility can be controlled parametrically via stimulus duration and masking
Reproduction responses reflect the perceptual estimate of orientation
manipulations: Gabor orientation relative to cardinal axes
key_findings: Observers reproduced orientations of very brief, low-visibility stimuli quite accurately
Illusory deviation from cardinals followed a non-monotonic pattern across visibility
Deviation magnitude was maximal at intermediate visibility, smaller at lowest and highest
interpretations: Low-visibility orientation processing is noisier yet paradoxically more faithful to the physical input
Categorical processing of sensory information depends on perceptual awareness
